contentBlocks.text: You should inform your colleagues of your current trained level & request further assistance from a suitably trained person when necessary.
This first level online course does not in any way qualify you to work directly upon the High Voltage systems on the vehicle.

This training course is designed to improve your knowledge of Electric Vehicles (EVs) and make you aware of the associated risks, so that you may exercise appropriate care for yourself and others when working near an Electric Vehicle.
Click on the arrows to explore the learning objectives
Please enter your details and click next
Site logistics, car parking and movements, valeting.
Click on the most relevant role to you.

This course will begin with a short quiz designed to help you recognise your existing knowledge and those areas on which you will need to focus during the training course. 

You can skip the knowledge check if you’d prefer to jump straight  into the course, however if you selected ‘Non Technical’ or ‘Semi Technical’ it might be a good idea to find out what you know, and what you don’t know.

Based on your experience, our recommendation is highlighted below:
Click to below to explore the learning objectives for this course.

State safety precautions to be taken before approaching and working around electric/hybrid vehicles

  State how to identify motor vehicle high energy cabling and associated components

State how the vehicle may be safely charged using an external source
Know how to work safely around electric/hybrid vehicles

State the hazards associated with high energy electricity

State the hazards that may be present when connecting and charging electric/hybrid vehicles
Understand the hazards around motor vehicle high energy electrical systems

State how to identify electric/hybrid vehicles

  Give examples of the electric/hybrid vehicles that are currently available

 Identify alternative fuel source vehicles

Know about the types of electric 
& hybrid vehicles available
Throughout this course, you’ll see some technical terms. If you need help understanding any of them, just click the glossary icon anytime for quick definitions.
What is an EV and how can we recognise one?

Identifying features of electric vehicles will differ between manufacturers.This means that you may need to exercise your skill and experience to make sure that you know what type of vehicle you are looking at - getting it wrong could be risky.

What do you think you could look for to help you spot an EV?
What do you think you could look for to help you spot an EV?

Identifying features of electric vehicles will differ between manufacturers.This means that you may need to exercise your skill and experience to make sure that you know what type of vehicle you are looking at - getting it wrong could be risky.
It is important that you can recognise an electric vehicle when it comes into your workplace. You will then be ready to exercise the correct procedures that will be necessary for a safe visit.

Number Plate
Full EV may have a green border, (not mandatory)

Badging 
Many EVs will have a badge indicating that they are an electric vehicle

Charge Port
On Battery (BEV) and Plug-in Hybrids (PHEV) there will be a battery charge lead connector port somewhere on the vehicle
Rotate the vehicle using the slider

You can identify an EV by some key  features of the interior. Move the image around by clicking and dragging and click on the ‘i’ symbol to find out more. Click on the ‘Explore interior’ button when you are ready.
Click the image for a larger view
Here are some other indicators that the vehicle is an EV. Click on the image to see an enlarged version.
Other indicators that the vehicle is an EV.
Let’s walk through the steps for using the government website to identify a vehicle.
You can recognise an Electric Vehicle by looking out for the identifying criteria in the list below

Badging
External mains charging connection port
Instrument cluster warning and messages
Power flow/ energy use screen
Bright-coloured high voltage cables
Green band of number plate
Warning labels
However, not all types of EV will have the same identifiers.
Click NEXT to see which type of Electric Vehicle will show the identifying criteria from this list.

Powered solely by a diesel engine – no electric motor or battery for propulsion
Energy is produced by burning fuel in the engine and transmitted mechanically to the wheels
Emits CO₂ and other pollutants through the exhaust system
Typically noisier and less efficient than electric vehicles
Still common globally, but being phased out in many regions due to environmental regulations

M HEV – Mild Hybrid Electric Vehicle

Let’s take a closer look at the different kinds 
of vehicles within the main categories.

Uses a Combustion Engine

Can have a special type of electric motor/ generator (belt integrated starter generator) that can turn the combustion engine for starting & to provide extra power to supplement the ICE when pulling away or accelerating

Can not operate in fully electric mode, the engine will work together with the electric motor to drive the vehicle

Typically operates at 48V . This is below the recognised definition of "High Voltage"

The 48 volt system wiring is often coloured purple or blue
The 48 volt system wiring is often coloured purple or blue
Typically operates at 48V . This is below the recognised definition of "High Voltage"
Can not operate in fully electric mode, the engine will work together with the electric motor to drive the vehicle

Can have a special type of electric motor/ generator (belt integrated starter generator) that can turn the combustion engine for starting & to provide extra power to supplement the ICE when pulling away or accelerating

Plug-in refers to the fact that the vehicle High Voltage battery can be charged using an external mains charger if required

The vehicle will also have a combustion engine

Has a larger capacity high voltage battery compared to a non plug-in hybrid, increasing the distance that can be driven using battery only power

Some types will use a combination of the electric motor and ICE engine to driver the vehicle wheels (Parallel Hybrid)

Other hybrid types may use the petrol engine only for charging the battery. The battery then powers the electric motor to move the vehicle (Series Hybrid)

The high voltage system can be identified by the orange or brightly coloured cabling
The high voltage system can be identified by the orange or brightly coloured cabling
Other hybrid types may use the petrol engine only for charging the battery. The battery then powers the electric motor to move the vehicle (Series Hybrid)
Some types will use a combination of the electric motor and ICE engine to driver the vehicle wheels (Parallel Hybrid)
Has a larger capacity high voltage battery compared to a non plug-in hybrid, increasing the distance that can be driven using battery only power
The vehicle will also have a combustion engine
Plug-in refers to the fact that the vehicle High Voltage battery can be charged using an external mains charger if required

Usually fitted with an Internal Combustion Engine (ICE), High Voltage battery and electric motor assembly

Some will use their electric motor simultaneously with the ICE to drive the wheels

When the engine and electric motor work together this is called a Parallel Hybrid mode

Other hybrids can use their petrol engine to charge the battery and never use it to move the vehicle. This is called a Series Hybrid mode

Some vehicles are able to switch between these 2 modes- vehicles that can do this are called  Series/ Parallel Hybrids

The high voltage system can be identified by the orange or brightly coloured cabling

Has a large capacity High voltage battery to provide the required driving range

Instant driving torque from the motor gives strong performance with no engine noise

Hover over the circles for the advantages and disadvantages
Good compromise between range and clean running. Optimised use of both powertrains can lead to very high MPG figures.
Extra cost of EV systems means these vehicles are often more expensive than petrol or diesel equivalents.

Cost of EV systems means theyse vehicles are  more expensive than petrol or diesel equivalents.  Smaller battery capacity means less Electric only range, as not often able to be charged using mains electricity.

Petrol or diesel engine removes the worry of running out of battery charge.  
Engine and electric motor combination can be used as necessary to drive the vehicle and charge the battery in the most efficient way.

Cost of EV systems means these vehicles are  more expensive than petrol or diesel equivalents.
The wheels cannot be driven by the petrol/diesel  engine at all, so a very low battery will need to continuously run the engine to recharge. 
Smaller battery capacity means less electric motor only range , as not often able to be charged using mains electricity.
Good compromise between range and clean running. Petrol or diesel engine removes the worry of running out of battery charge.

Cost of EV systems means these vehicles are  more expensive than petrol or diesel equivalents.
The wheels cannot be driven by the petrol/diesel  engine at all, so a very low battery will need to continuously run the engine to recharge. 
Smaller battery capacity means less electric motor only range , as not often able to be charged using mains electricity.
Good compromise between range and clean running. Petrol or diesel engine removes the worry of running out of battery charge.

Cost of EV systems means these vehicles are  more expensive than petrol or diesel equivalents.  
Smaller battery capacity means less Electric only range, as not often able to be charged using mains electricity.

Petrol or diesel engine removes the worry of running out of battery charge. 
Engine and electric motor combination can be used as necessary to drive in the most efficient way.

Stretches the definition of "Hybrid". 
No purely electric drive available.
Another name for "stop/start" technology - very cheap and basic fuel saving solution.

Does not support purely electric drive. 
Components are more expensive than conventional types (alternator v starter generator costs).

Saves fuel and reduces emissions using a starter generator system. 
Comparatively easy for a manufacturer to engineer this technology  into existing vehicle types.

Cost of EV systems means these vehicles are  more expensive than petrol or diesel equivalents.
Battery driven, electric motor only range will still be less than a fully electric EV due to smaller battery sizes.

Offers both range and clean running. Larger battery than non plug-in hybrids, giving greater EV only range. Petrol or diesel engine removes the worry of running out of battery charge. 
Can be charged from the mains electricity supply.
Extra cost of EV systems means these vehicles are often  more expensive than petrol or diesel equivalents. Battery only range will be less than a fully electric EV due to smaller battery sizes.
Good compromise between range and clean running. Optimised use of both powertrains can lead to very high MPG figures.
Reliance on charging network, battery repair costs compared to ICE repair costs.

No tailpipe emissions, greatest electric only driving range of all EV types. 
Easy to drive.

Expensive and rare at present (2025). 
Fueling infrastructure for the hydrogen that they need is not widely available in most countries.

Very clean running. 
Long range.
Fuel cell vehicles have zero tailpipe emissions, producing only clean water vapour as hydrogen reacts with oxygen in the fuel cell to generate electricity.

Tailpipe emissions, noise pollution. 
Due to be phased out in various countries.
No cost of batteries, No cost of EV high voltage  control systems. Good range from a full tank of fuel. Quick to refuel, fuel station availability.

100^%^ electric motor drive
External fuel based power unit drives a generator to charge the battery & extend the vehicle range
High voltage battery can be charged by an external mains charger, hence the "Plug-in” hybrid name

Internal combustion engine & electric motor
Smaller high voltage battery capacity

Driven 100^%^ by electric motor
Fuel cell converts hydrogen and oxygen into electric power

Driven 100^%^ by electric motor
Large capacity rechargeable high voltage battery
Let’s test your knowledge on this topic so far with a few questions.
Hover over the components for a definiton. Click on the button below to show the bodywork of a Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle.
Hydrogen fuel filler – Allows hydrogen to be safely pumped into the vehicle’s onboard hydrogen tank during refuelling
Hydrogen storage tanks – Store compressed hydrogen gas safely onboard the vehicle to supply the fuel cell system when needed
Fuel cell system – Converts hydrogen gas into electricity through a chemical reaction with oxygen, powering the electric motor and charging the battery
High voltage traction battery- Voltage approx. >60-800 v battery, powers the electric drive motors
Power Electronics controller- contains the inverter to change AC to DC current and vice- versa, and the electronic controller that controls the power flow of the vehicle
Electric drive motor - Powered by high voltage AC from the Electronics controller to drive the vehicle. In some cases can also work as a motor/ generator
DC - DC Converter- Changes high voltage from the traction battery to low voltage for the vehicle low voltage systems battery
Battery cooling system - keeps the battery cool

We hope you now have the information that you need to allow you to recognise an EV when it comes into your place of work.

Click on the next button to begin a short quiz.
Hazards around high voltage electrical systems

At the end of this session you should be able to:

State the hazards associated with high energy electricity 
State the hazards that may be present when connecting and charging electric/hybrid vehicles

Voltages present in E&HVs are significantly higher, currently up to 650 Volts direct current (DC)) than  those used in other vehicles (12/24 Volts DC). In dry conditions, accidental contact with parts that are  live at voltages above 110 Volts DC can be fatal. 
Battery systems may contain chemicals that can be harmful if released. They also store significant  amounts of energy that can give rise to explosion if not dealt with correctly.

Introduction to high voltages

The following information is taken form the UK HSE website guidelines for electric vehicles.
‘People in the motor vehicle repair and recovery industry are now more likely to come across Electric & Hybrid Vehicles and as a result need to be aware of the additional hazards they may be exposed to  when working with these vehicles.
They may also need to develop a wider range of skills and knowledge and have access to specialist  tools and equipment in order to be able to work safely.
Definition ECE R100 currently describes high voltage as:

"High Voltage" means the classification of an electric component or circuit, if it’s working voltage is between >60V 
and ≤1500VDC or >30V and ≤1000VAC root mean square (rms).

These values are used when referring to Electric Vehicles. 
Please note that high voltage is defined  differently in other industry sectors.

A UN regulation, ECE R100 certification, is one of the most important European requirements for the  approval of electric road vehicles, because the regulation specifies all the tests that must be  performed on batteries installed in electric vehicles of category N and M with electric traction.

slides.title: Risks of working with Electric & Hybrid vehicles

contentBlocks.text: Risks of working with Electric & Hybrid vehicles

EVs & HVs introduce hazards into the workplace in addition to those normally associated with the repair and maintenance of vehicles, roadside recovery and other vehicle related activities. These include:

The potential for the release of explosive gases and harmful liquids & chemicals if batteries are  damaged or incorrectly modified. 

The possibility of people being unaware of vehicles moving as when electrically driven they are  silent in operation. 

The potential for the electrical systems on the vehicle to affect medical devices such as  pacemakers.
The potential for the electrical systems on the vehicle to affect medical devices such as  pacemakers.
The possibility of people being unaware of vehicles moving as when electrically driven they are  silent in operation.
The potential for the release of explosive gases and harmful liquids & chemicals if batteries are  damaged or incorrectly modified.

The presence of high voltage components and cabling capable of delivering a fatal electric shock. The storage of electrical energy with the potential to cause explosion or fire. Stored electrical energy can be sufficient to cause Arc/ flash.

Components that may retain a dangerous voltage even when a vehicle is switched off. Electric motors or the vehicle itself that may move unexpectedly due to magnetic forces within the  motors. 

Manual handling risks associated with battery replacement. Hybrid vehicles also have a normal fuel tank present on the vehicle.
Manual handling risks associated with battery replacement. Hybrid vehicles also have a normal fuel tank present on the vehicle.
Components that may retain a dangerous voltage even when a vehicle is switched off. Electric motors or the vehicle itself that may move unexpectedly due to magnetic forces within the  motors.

The presence of high voltage components and cabling capable of delivering a fatal electric shock. The storage of electrical energy with the potential to cause explosion or fire. Stored electrical energy can be sufficient to cause Arc/ flash.

slides.title: Considerations: What work are we doing?

contentBlocks.text:
The UK Health and Safety Executive identifies 4 categories of work for Electric & High voltage  vehicles. These are: 

Valeting, sales and other lower risk activities 
Incident response including emergency services and vehicle recovery 
Maintenance and repair excluding high voltage electrical systems 
Working on high voltage electrical systems
Considerations for Valeting, sales and other lower risk activities:

Similarly, people who work around Electric & Hybrid vehicles should be aware that the vehicles may not have a normal engine sound when moving. Note that most EVs will be fitted with a pedestrian sound generator warning device, known as AVAS (Acoustic Vehicle Alerting System), but the sound it generates may not be what you expect to hear.

Pressure washing has the potential to damage high voltage electrical components and  cables. High voltage cables are usually coloured orange. Refer to guidance from  manufacturers before valeting in any under body areas including the engine bay.

Pressure washing has the potential to damage high voltage electrical components and  cables. High voltage cables are usually coloured orange. Refer to guidance from  manufacturers before valeting in any under body areas including the engine bay.

Similarly, people who work around Electric & Hybrid vehicles should be aware that the vehicles may not have a normal engine sound when moving. Note that most EVs will be fitted with a pedestrian sound generator warning device, known as AVAS (Acoustic Vehicle Alerting System), but the sound it generates may not be what you expect to hear.

Remote operation keys that only need to be close to the vehicle for the vehicle to be powered  up should be kept away from vehicles. This is to prevent the vehicle from accidentally being switched on. 

People who move these vehicles around the workplace should be aware that others may not  hear you approaching them.
People who move these vehicles around the workplace should be aware that others may not  hear you approaching them.

Remote operation keys that only need to be close to the vehicle for the vehicle to be powered  up should be kept away from vehicles. This is to prevent the vehicle from accidentally being switched on.

This training course does not replace dedicated First Aid & Emergency training. 
It is designed to give an overview of potential injuries and the immediate action required. In all instances of accident and injury the advice of a medical professional should be sought as soon as possible.

A voltage as low as 50 volts applied between two parts of the human body causes a current to flow that can block the electrical signals between the brain and the muscles. This may have a number of effects including:
Secondary injuries are also likely, (burns, fall injuries)

The severity of a shock is determined by a combination of factors.
Click on the example buttons below to find out more.
What to do if you encounter someone receiving an electric shock.

Assess the situation

Do not touch the casualty if they are still in contact with the electrical source as you are at risk of electrocution.
Click on each of the steps below to find out what you should do if you encounter someone receiving an electric shock.

To prevent further casualties, ensure that no one else attempts to work on the vehicle until it has been made safe. It should be cordoned off using a non-conductive safety barrier chain, and the risk assessed before any further work is undertaken.

Once you are sure that the contact has been broken between the casualty and the high voltage electrical source, perform a primary survey and treat any injuries, seeking assistance if available on site, such as the company first aid person.

Therefore, if the casualty is still in contact with the electrical source and it is safe for you to do so, use a purpose-designed insulated rescue hook to separate them from the power supply. The rescue hook should be kept visible, within easy reach and access at the electrical vehicle charging bay at all times, ready for use in an emergency. If you are not confident or trained in its use, then do not attempt to use any other untested rescue methods.

In the case of high voltage vehicles and batteries, in normal circumstances the high voltage supply will be disconnected when turning the vehicle off using the normal key. HOWEVER-You should consider that there may be a fault with the vehicle that could endanger you if you attempt to do this without first understanding what the cause of the problem is, i.e. why the person is receiving a shock in the first place?

For mains electrical systems, such as the vehicle battery chargers used for BEV & PHEV, you should turn off the power supply at the isolation switch before attempting any action if possible.  

Do you know where this is located for your workplace?

On the next screen, match the risk to the relevant operating condition or system.
Drag each red box (risk) onto the white box (system) it most accurately relates to.
This will help you understand the safety implications of each electric vehicle feature.
Can be ready to drive without a running engine
Silent running when using electric motor
Engine automatic control for stop start
High Voltage components, battery, cables, generator, etc
Unexpected movement when gas pedal is used
Pedestrians and co workers may not notice moving vehicles
Vehicle may start unexpectedly (this could be especially dangerous for technicians)

That’s right – you selected the correct responses. Let’s look at how to mitigate these risks on the next screen. Click next to continue.
Unexpected movement when gas pedal is used
Pedestrians and co workers may not notice moving vehicles
Vehicle may start unexpectedly (this could be especially dangerous for technicians)
Pay attention to warnings and messages on the instrument cluster showing vehicle drive status
Be vigilant when driving electric vehicles near to other people, ensure acoustic warning system is functioning correctly
Remove keys to prevent vehicle starting
Isolate high voltage system to prevent starting
Disconnect low voltage system to prevent starting
Set out signage to let others know that this is an EV
Cordon off vehicles being worked on to prevent unauthorised access
Isolate high voltage system to prevent the possibility of electric shock
Disconnect low voltage system to prevent electric shock
Ensure that only suitably qualified persons work on the high voltage system
Drag and drop the solutions into the correct drop zone

That’s incorrect – you did not select the correct responses. 
Click on the ‘retry’ button or ‘watch video again’.
That’s right – you selected the correct responses. Let’s look at how to mitigate these risks on the next screen. Click next to continue.
Click on the buttons below to learn more:

Watts refers to the energy consumption of a component. 
We are familiar with this in terms of bulbs and other household items.

Amps is the measurement used to work out electrical flow
There is a relationship between voltage, amps and the resistance in a circuit, the latter of which is created by a consumer
A higher amp reading indicates a larger current is flowing
Watts also have a relationship with the above measurements

Voltage is the term used to describe electrical pressure
ICE passenger vehicles use 12v. Trucks and buses usually use 24v systems
Mild Hybrids 48V
Hybrids 200-400 Typically
Full EV 350-800V Typically

The 2.3Kw described on the previous page is calculated by multiplying the power output (Watts) of the house (230W) by the Amps that the cables can carry (Typically 10Amps).

Wall boxes fitted to commercial premises and homes can run higher, typically between 7.4kw and 22kw.

Rapid chargers are typically 50kw plus with the fastest in the U.K being 360Kw at time of writing, although vehicles aren’t able to charge at this rate at present.

We can reverse this information when it comes to charging.
“My Home can charge the car at 2.3Kw”

So in principle to charge the previously mentioned 52KW battery I would need to divide 52Kw by 2.3Kw to work out the time to charge it fully, resulting in 22.6hrs* to fully charge at home.

The reason we need to understand these values is because the are mentioned when it comes to system operation and charging.

“The car has a 52KWH battery”

This basically means you can run a 52000 Watt consumer for an Hour before the battery goes flat.

If this was 77KWH then we could run a 77000 Watt consumer for an Hour or run the 52000 Watt consumer for almost 1.5 hrs before the battery goes flat.
There will now be a 10 question revision, covering areas from each session

Arc/Flash
A sudden release of electrical energy through the air when a high-voltage gap is breached. Arc flash can cause serious burns, injuries, or equipment damage.
Battery Cooling System
A system designed to keep the battery within a safe operating temperature.
BEV (Battery Electric Vehicle)
A vehicle powered entirely by an electric motor using energy stored in a battery. No internal combustion engine.
DC-DC Converter
Converts high voltage DC power from the battery to low voltage DC power (12V) for vehicle accessories.
Electric Drive Motor
An electric motor that propels the vehicle’s wheels.
External Mains Charge Connection Port
The socket on the vehicle where charging cables are connected.
Generator
In hybrid vehicles, a generator (often part of a motor-generator unit) converts mechanical energy from the engine into electrical energy to recharge the battery or assist propulsion.
HEV (Hybrid Electric Vehicle)
A vehicle combining a petrol engine and an electric motor. The electric motor assists the engine.
High Voltage (HV) System
Electrical components and wiring operating at high voltage levels (generally above 60V DC), which can be hazardous.
Insulated Gloves (Class 0 rated)
Protective gloves that provide insulation against electrical shock, rated for up to 1500 volts DC.
Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)
A petrol or diesel engine that creates mechanical power by burning fuel.
Key Signal Blocker Pouch/Case
A protective pouch that blocks wireless signals from car keys to prevent theft or interference.
MHEV (Mild Hybrid Electric Vehicle)
A vehicle with a small electric motor that assists the combustion engine but cannot power the vehicle alone.
On-board Charger (Built-in Mains Charger)
Device inside the vehicle that converts mains AC power to DC to charge the traction battery.
PHEV (Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle)
A hybrid vehicle with a larger battery that can be recharged by plugging into an external electric power source.
Power Electronics Controller
Device that manages electrical power between the battery and electric motor, including DC to AC conversion.
Rescue Hook
An insulated tool used by emergency responders to safely pull a person away from high-voltage components in the event of electric shock.
Traction Battery
The main battery providing power to the electric motor in an EV.
UN Regulation ECE R100
International safety standard for electrical safety in electric vehicles.
